title: SEGMENT 5 — Pharmacological vocabulary: how we measure killing

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Pharmacological vocabulary: how we measure killing
Before we touch a single drug, I need to give you the vocabulary pharmacologists use to describe what an antibiotic does, quantitatively.
These terms will come up over and over, and they're genuinely the language of the field.
First distinction: bacteriostatic versus bactericidal.
A bacteriostatic drug stops bacteria from growing and dividing — it freezes the population.
It doesn't actively kill them; it holds them in check, and then the patient's own immune system comes in and mops up the now-helpless, non-replicating bacteria.
A bactericidal drug, by contrast, actively kills the bacteria itself.
The population count goes down because the drug is doing the killing.
Now, why does this matter clinically?
For most ordinary infections in a person with a working immune system, bacteriostatic is perfectly fine — you just need to freeze the bugs and let the immune system finish the job.

But in certain situations, you really want bactericidal activity: in a patient whose immune system is compromised, or in infections where the immune system can't easily reach, like endocarditis — an infection on a heart valve — or meningitis, or osteomyelitis in bone.
In those settings, you can't rely on the host to do the killing, so you want a drug that does it for you.
Here's a subtlety I want you to hold loosely, though: bacteriostatic versus bactericidal is not a fixed, intrinsic label stamped on a drug forever.
It depends on the organism, the drug concentration, and the conditions.
A drug can be static against one bug and cidal against another.
So treat these as useful tendencies, not iron laws.
But as tendencies, they correlate beautifully with mechanism, and I'll point that out as we go.
As a preview: drugs that break the cell wall or shatter the membrane tend to be cidal, because they cause physical catastrophe.
Drugs that gently inhibit protein synthesis often tend to be static, because if you remove the drug, the ribosome just starts working again.
There are exceptions — there are always exceptions — but the logic holds often enough to be worth knowing.
Second concept: the MIC, the minimum inhibitory concentration.
This is probably the single most important number in clinical antibiotic use.
The MIC is the lowest concentration of a drug that prevents visible growth of a particular bacterial strain.
You take the bug, you expose it to a series of drug concentrations, and you find the lowest one that stops it growing.
That's your MIC.
A low MIC means the drug is potent against that organism — you don't need much.
A high MIC means you need a lot, or that the drug is barely working.
When a lab reports that an organism is "susceptible" or "resistant" to a drug, underneath that report is an MIC being compared to a threshold.
There's a sister measurement, the MBC, the minimum bactericidal concentration — the lowest concentration that actually kills, rather than just inhibits, 99.9% of the organisms.
For a strongly bactericidal drug, the MBC is close to the MIC.
For a bacteriostatic drug, the MBC is way higher than the MIC — you can inhibit it easily but you'd need enormous concentrations to actually kill it.
So the gap between MIC and MBC is itself a clue about whether a drug is static or cidal against that organism.

Now the third and most pharmacologically interesting concept, and this is where it gets elegant: there are two fundamentally different patterns by which antibiotics kill, and they have completely opposite dosing strategies.
This is one of those ideas that separates someone who's memorized drugs from someone who actually understands them.
Some drugs are concentration-dependent killers.
For these, the higher the peak concentration you achieve relative to the MIC, the more effectively and the faster they kill.
More is better.
The relevant pharmacodynamic parameter is the ratio of the peak concentration to the MIC — we write it Cmax over MIC — or the total drug exposure over the day relative to MIC, the AUC over MIC.
For these drugs, the dosing logic is: give a big, high dose, get a huge peak, kill aggressively, and then you can actually let the level fall quite low before the next dose.
We'll see that aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones behave this way, and it's why aminoglycosides are now usually given as one big dose once a day rather than split into small doses.
The other group are time-dependent killers.
For these, cranking the concentration higher does not kill faster — once you're a few times above the MIC, you've saturated the effect, and pushing higher buys you nothing.
What matters for these drugs is time: the fraction of the dosing interval during which the drug concentration stays above the MIC.
We write that as T greater than MIC, "time above MIC.
" For these drugs, the dosing logic is the opposite: you don't want a giant peak and a long trough; you want to keep the level above the MIC for as much of the day as possible.
That means frequent dosing, or even continuous infusion.
The beta-lactams — penicillins and cephalosporins — are the classic time-dependent killers, which is exactly why penicillin is dosed several times a day rather than once.
So notice what just happened: two drugs, two opposite dosing philosophies, and the difference comes straight out of the killing mechanism.
That's the pharmacology talking.
One more term and then we start on real drugs: the post-antibiotic effect, or PAE.
This is the observation that for some drugs, bacterial growth stays suppressed for a while even after the drug concentration has dropped below the MIC.
The drug is essentially gone, but the bugs are still stunned and don't resume growing for hours.

Aminoglycosides have a long post-antibiotic effect, and this is the second reason you can give them once a day — the killing is done by the big peak, and the prolonged post-antibiotic effect covers the long trough while the level is low.
Mechanism driving dosing, again.
And finally, the word spectrum.
A broad-spectrum antibiotic works against a wide range of bacterial types — gram-positives, gram-negatives, maybe more.
A narrow-spectrum antibiotic targets a limited range.
Broad sounds better, but it isn't always — broad-spectrum drugs do more collateral damage to your normal, helpful bacteria, which is how you end up with complications like C. difficile colitis.
The ideal is to use the narrowest drug that covers the actual pathogen.
Hold that thought; it'll matter when we talk about resistance at the end.
Okay.
Vocabulary done.
You now have the measuring tools.
Let's go build some drugs — or rather, let's go break some bacteria.
We start with the cell wall, because it's the biggest difference and the most famous drugs live there.
